fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the reflexive subgraph comprehension \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, B \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} is a path object.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x of reflexivity data to each vertex.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph. A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} over \mathcal {A} is given by families of types \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} : b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b for every u:Bx.

Given a displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} and a vertex x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we shall write \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} for the component of \mathcal {B} at x:A, which we define to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} x \\ u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} } v &: \equiv u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } v \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} } {u} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} x \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } v \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph with vertices in a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \approx _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } {x} : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a reflexive graph structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } x \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \times \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} \times \mathopen {} \left ( u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} } v \right ) \mathclose {} \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} } {u} \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} be a family of reflexive graphs indexed in a type A. We define the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ f \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } g &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } f x \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } g x \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } {f} &: \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the reflexive subgraph comprehension \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, B \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} is a path object.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.
The product of a family \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a reflexive graph structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } x \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
The correspondence between biinvertible and bijective maps of and lifts to an equivalence from the type of equivalences to the type of bijections.
By and via .
